Thanks for joining the review, Milton. Walk me through the quarter, how did the spring collection do?
Strong, actually. It came in under budget, so we have a surplus, and the forecast for next quarter looks good, around fifteen percent growth.
That is great. And the overhead? Last time the shipping expenses were a worry.
Still high, it is eating into the profit. Honestly, if we had allocated more to logistics on the winter orders, we would have kept the margin.
Agreed. So what is the plan? Look at the cash flow on the projection before you answer.
I would allocate the surplus to shipping up front. If we protect the margin now, next quarter profit will hold.
If we had cut the overhead earlier, the profit would have been higher.
Let us allocate the surplus to logistics and watch the cash flow.
If we had allocated more to shipping, we would have kept the margin.
Let us allocate the surplus to logistics to protect the margin.

Hi Milton, it is Karen, just leaving you a quick voice note before our quarterly review tomorrow so the numbers are not a surprise. The good news first: revenue is up, and the spring collection came in under budget, which gives us a healthy surplus to reinvest. The forecast for next quarter looks strong too, around fifteen percent growth if the new pieces sell the way we expect. The one thing I want to flag is overhead. The shipping and the customs expenses crept up again, and they are eating into the profit on every order. I think if we allocate a little more to logistics up front, we will protect the margin later. Take a look at the projection I sent, especially the cash flow on page two, and come ready to talk about where we trim and where we invest. No pressure, I just know how far your English has come, and honestly I am looking forward to a real strategy conversation. Talk tomorrow.

Hey, this is Milton, a quick voice note to myself after the quarterly review. It went well, but it made me think about last year. If I had read the forecast more carefully back then, I would have seen the overhead problem coming months earlier. If we had allocated more to shipping from the start, we would not have lost so much margin on the winter orders, and the profit would have looked very different. Honestly, if I had spoken better English a year ago, I would have pushed back on that supplier contract instead of just nodding along. The good thing is, today I can actually have this conversation. I read the projection, I questioned the expenses, and I explained the cash flow to Karen without freezing once. The takeaway is simple. The numbers were never really the hard part. The hard part was finding the words, and now I have them. Next quarter, if a forecast looks off, I will say so on the call, in the moment, in English.
